nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Tanganyika; found over sandy-rocky substrate in shallow to mid-depth areas; shelters and spawns in rock crevices and caves

decorPreferences: extensive rockwork with caves and crevices for spawning and territory
minimal or no planting (not required; Tanganyika biotope)
open swimming areas between rock structures

diet.notes: In the wild, the Convict Julie picks invertebrates and small crustaceans from rocks and sandy substrate. In captivity it generally accepts high-quality cichlid pellets or flakes, but live and frozen foods — mosquito larvae, mysis shrimp, daphnia, and baby brine shrimp — should make up a large share of the diet. Blanched spinach or spirulina can provide variety, but meaty foods remain the priority. Fatty mammalian meats should be avoided.

summary: The Convict Julie (Julidochromis regani) is the largest species in its genus, reaching up to 15 cm and endemic to Lake Tanganyika. It forms a lifelong monogamous pair bond and occurs in several geographic color forms. The species requires hard, alkaline water and ample rockwork, and is suited to intermediate-level keepers.
careNotes: Julidochromis regani is the largest species in the Julidochromis genus, with females reaching up to 15 cm total length — noticeably larger than the closely related J. marlieri (fw-fish-110, which displays a grid pattern and stays smaller) and J. transcriptus (fw-fish-197, the smallest Julie). Like all Julies, it is endemic to Lake Tanganyika and demands hard, alkaline water (pH 8.0–9.0, gH 10–25, kH 10–25); soft or acidic conditions should be avoided entirely. A tank of at least 110 L with a 120 cm footprint is the recommended minimum for a single bonded pair. Rockwork is essential — the species retreats into crevices for shelter, territory defence, and spawning; without adequate caves the fish will become highly stressed. Sand or fine gravel substrate is appropriate. Multiple geographic color forms are collected and traded, including the Kipili form (3 thin black stripes on yellow; now sometimes assigned to the closely related J. marksmithi), Kerenge (3 stripes on bright yellow), Kigoma (4–5 thick stripes on cream), Burundi (4 thick stripes on cream), Kachese, and Sumbu/Zambia variants. Yellow forms tend to come from shallower, sandy-rocky areas; darker cream-based variants from deeper rocky zones. Only one Julidochromis species (or geographic form) should be kept per aquarium, as the genus hybridises readily — crosses between J. regani, J. marlieri, J. transcriptus, and Chalinochromis are well documented. The Convict Julie is best kept as a bonded pair; a single specimen may be bullied or may harass tankmates. Compatible Tanganyikan tankmates include Altolamprologus, Cyprichromis, Paracyprichromis, and Synodontis catfish. Feed primarily live and frozen invertebrate foods (mosquito larvae, mysis, daphnia, brine shrimp), with quality cichlid pellets or flakes as a staple. Perform regular partial water changes of 20–30% weekly to maintain the high mineral content and low organic load typical of Lake Tanganyika.
breedingNotes: The Convict Julie forms a strong, lifelong monogamous pair bond, with both sexes participating in territory defence and fry guarding. The pair spawns secretively inside rock crevices or caves, with the female depositing up to 200–300 adhesive eggs on the cave ceiling or walls. Both parents guard the clutch aggressively. Eggs hatch in approximately 3 days; the larvae become free-swimming after a further 4–5 days and are large enough to accept newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and powdered fry food immediately. Fry typically shelter near the parent pair and remain under their protection until approximately 2–3 cm in length, after which they are driven off. Growth is relatively slow; juveniles reach roughly 2.5 cm in about 4–5 months. Pairs are best established by purchasing a group of 4–6 juveniles and allowing natural pairing — once a pair forms, the remaining fish should be removed to prevent aggression. Breeding is moderately challenging: water chemistry must remain stable and within Tanganyikan parameters, but an established pair will generally spawn without strong intervention.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Julidochromis regani
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (rarely exceeds 15 cm in aquaria), temperature 23–27°C, pH 7.5–9.0, hardness 10–25°H, minimum tank 120 cm × 30 cm × 30 cm (110 L) for a breeding pair, diet (live/frozen majority with dry and vegetable supplement), endemic to Lake Tanganyika, spawning in caves with parental care, compatibility with Altolamprologus and Cyprichromis, hybridisation risk with other Julidochromis.

additional.name: FishBase — Julidochromis regani
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 13.0 cm TL), temperature 22–25°C, pH 8.5–9.2, native range Lake Tanganyika (3°S–9°S), habitat (shallows over sand/rocks, to ~5 m depth), family Cichlidae, order Cichliformes, oviparous biology, trophic level 3.6. Size discrepancy with SeriouslyFish (13 cm vs 15 cm) resolved by using 15 cm per aquainfo.nl female data and SeriouslyFish wild note.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Julidochromis regani
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (males ~12 cm, females 14–15 cm), temperature 24–26°C, pH 8.5–9.0, gH 7–11, minimum tank 150 cm footprint, six geographic color forms (Burundi, Kachese, Kerenge, Kigoma, Kipili, Zambia/Sumbu), monogamous substrate-spawning pair, up to 200 eggs, lifespan not stated.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Convict Julie
additional.notes: Confirmed lifespan 5–8 years, pH 7.5–9.5, temperature 77–79°F (25–26°C), native range across DRC/Burundi/Tanzania/Zambia, careLevel intermediate to experienced, monogamous pairing, substrate spawner with biparental care, hybridisation risk with Chalinochromis and Telmatochromis.

additional.name: GCCA — Julidochromis regani ‘Kipili’ Profile
additional.notes: Confirmed pair-bonding monogamy, substrate/cave spawning, clutch size 35–150 eggs, slow fry growth (~5 months to 1 cm), geographic color adaptation (yellow in shallows, cream-darker at depth), captive diet acceptance (cichlid flakes, NLS, baby brine shrimp).

fish.breedingNotes: The Convict Julie is a monogamous substrate-spawner that deposits eggs in rock crevices or caves, with both parents actively guarding the eggs and fry. Clutches typically contain up to 200–300 eggs, and newly free-swimming fry are large enough to accept brine shrimp nauplii immediately. Pairs are best established by raising a juvenile group of four to six fish together and removing extra individuals once a pair has formed.
